evidence_quotes.quote: Right now, publishers canand often docompletely revoke access to games people have bought by shutting down servers or online components, leaving players with nothing.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, I want to voice my strongest support for the Digital Fairness Act and ask you to ensure that video games are recognized as digital products deserving the same consumer protections as any other purchased good. Right now, publishers canand often docompletely revoke access to games people have bought by shutting down servers or online components, leaving players with nothing. There is no fallback, no way to preserve what we paid for. A product worth 60 or 70 euros can vanish overnight, not because its broken, but because a company decides its no longer profitable to keep it alive. As both a game developer who has worked with studios like CD Projekt Red and GSC Game World, and as a lifelong gamer, I find this terrifying. Video games are more than softwarethey are culture, art, and memories we share with friends and family. When publishers turn off a game, they are not just removing code, they are erasing experiences that people have paid for and cherished. Ive personally lost games I loved this way. Anthem, which will soon disappear forever. Assassins Creed Brotherhood, whose online features Ubisoft discontinued in 2022. Burnout Paradise, The Crew, and countless others that have been taken from players despite being fully functional. Every time it happens, it feels like someone breaking into your home and burning a book you bought because the publisher decided you shouldnt read it anymore. The Stop Killing Games initiative, which has gained over 1.4 million signatures, shows just how deeply this issue affects people across Europe. Its call for mandatory end-of-life plans and alternative offline or community-run solutions is not radicalits common sense. Consumers deserve assurance that what they pay for today will not simply vanish tomorrow. I am grateful for the Digital Fairness Act and the effort to make digital spaces safer and more balanced. Please, extend its protection to video games and make it clear: once sold, a game belongs to the customer. This would not only protect consumers but also preserve an art form and strengthen trust between players and the industry. Thank you for considering this and for fighting for a fairer digital future for all of us. With respect
